title: Classical CV's Lasting Role
concept: Classical CV isn't obsolete — hand-designed techniques still matter for preprocessing, data augmentation with geometric transforms, and fast lightweight tasks where a full neural network would be overkill.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How does a neural network get its own filter values?

options.text: Backpropagation adjusts them automatically based on training errors
An engineer types the exact numbers in by hand
They're copied directly from a Sobel filter every time

exercises.explain: Right — learned filters come from backpropagation, not hand-design. So is classical CV finished? Not quite.

cards.title: Old tools, still in the toolbox
cards.body: Deep learning didn't erase classical computer vision — it just gave it a new job. Open up nearly any modern CV pipeline today and you'll still find blur, resizing, and geometric transforms quietly doing essential work.

cards.title: Where classical CV still matters
cards.body: Classical CV techniques remain essential for three big jobs: preprocessing, cleaning an image up before it reaches a network; data augmentation, using geometric transforms to create more training variety; and lightweight tasks, simple jobs where a full neural network would be slow overkill.

cards.title: A hand tool next to a power tool
cards.body: You wouldn't fire up a power saw to tighten one small screw. Sometimes a simple hand-crafted filter is the right-sized tool — fast, predictable, and cheap — even in a world that now has powerful learned models available.

cards.title: Augmentation before training
cards.body: Before training a deep network on a dataset of cat photos, engineers use classical geometric transforms — rotating, flipping, scaling, cropping — to multiply their training data and make the network more robust to angle and size changes, no learning required for that step.

cards.title: Learned doesn't mean 'always better'
cards.body: It's easy to assume a learned model always beats a classical one. But for a simple task like resizing a thumbnail, blurring a face for privacy, or basic denoising, a hand-crafted filter finishes in microseconds with zero training — a neural network there would be needless overhead.

left.text:
Complex pattern recognition
e.g. 'is this a cat or a dog?'

exercises.prompt: Which is a real reason classical CV techniques are still used today?

options.text: They're used for preprocessing, data augmentation, and lightweight tasks where a full network is overkill
Neural networks are legally required to run a classical filter first
Classical filters are always more accurate than any trained network
Modern cameras can't produce images without them

exercises.explain: Classical CV earns its place through speed and simplicity, not by out-accuracy-ing deep learning at complex tasks.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Geometric transforms like rotating and flipping images are still commonly used to augment training data for deep learning models.
exercises.explain: True. Rotating, flipping, and cropping training images is a standard, classical way to make deep learning models more robust.

exercises.sentence: A hand-crafted blur filter is still a good choice for a simple, fast task where training a full neural network would be ___.
exercises.explain: Overkill is correct — a classical filter can do a simple job in microseconds with no training needed.

exercises.prompt: Which task is best suited to a classical filter rather than a full deep learning model?

options.text: Quickly blurring a thumbnail image for a privacy preview
Recognizing 1,000 different dog breeds from a single photo
Reading the emotional tone of a handwritten paragraph

exercises.explain: A quick, fixed operation like blurring a thumbnail is exactly where a lightweight classical filter shines.

exercises.prompt: A team is building an app that (1) recognizes 500 different plant species from photos, and (2) needs to quickly rotate and crop training images to expand their dataset. Which technique fits which job?

options.text: Deep learning for species recognition, since it's complex pattern recognition; classical geometric transforms for the fast augmentation step
Classical filters for species recognition, and deep learning for rotating and cropping images
Deep learning should handle both tasks from scratch, including rotating and cropping
Classical filters should handle both tasks, including recognizing all 500 species

exercises.explain: Exactly — reach for deep learning where the pattern is genuinely complex, and lean on fast classical tools for the simple, mechanical steps around it. Next up, the big picture: how everything since pixels connects to what comes after this phase.
